summary: Cathy O'Neil reflects on her March 2020 prediction that the coronavirus pandemic would trigger a massive political uprising. While other 'thought leaders' predicted optimistic outcomes like reduced polarization and renewed faith in experts, O'Neil foresaw that the unequal response to the crisis would expose how wealthy communities would be protected while poor communities would be devastated. She argues that the pandemic revealed how massive political action is possible when causes are deemed urgent, making the chronic dismissal of marginalized populations' needs impossible to ignore. Writing in July 2020, she expresses sadness at being proven correct.
key_points: Other experts predicted optimistic post-pandemic outcomes like less polarization and renewed trust in science
The pandemic response would reveal a stark divide between how wealthy vs. poor communities were treated
Multitrillion-dollar bailouts proved that rapid, massive political action is possible when deemed urgent
The combination of long-ignored needs and visible proof of political capacity would radicalize marginalized populations
Being proven right about social upheaval brings no satisfaction
money_quotes: The aftermath of the coronavirus is likely to include a new political uprising—an Occupy Wall Street 2.0, but this time much more massive and angrier.

We will see the extent to which rich, well-connected and well-resourced communities will have been taken care of, while contingent, poor and stigmatized communities will have been thoroughly destroyed.
We will have seen how political action is possible—multitrillion dollar bailouts and projects can be mobilized quickly—but only if the cause is considered urgent.

This mismatch of long-disregarded populations finally getting the message that their needs are not only chronically unattended, but also chronically dismissed as politically required, will likely have drastic, pitchfork consequences.
It makes me sad to feel so right about this.
key_insight: The pandemic exposed that governments can mobilize massive resources instantly when motivated, revealing the chronic dismissal of marginalized communities' needs as a political choice rather than an impossibility.
